coaching.coaching: Plan how stale documentation references to eliminated function will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: stale documentation references to eliminated function

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace or augment the 'code review' hedge in AC2 with an automated assertion — e.g., extend the AST neutrality gate to assert that `outbound_links.py` references `opaque_vendor_type` only in payload-construction positions (or at minimum remove 'code review' as a stated proving method and rely solely on the behavior tests, explicitly documenting that as sufficient).
evidence: 'verified by code review + the behavior tests in Testing (identical ADD/REMOVE payloads to today)'
ABSENCE: no automated assertion checks that core never branches on opaque_vendor_type beyond carrying it in the payload; code review is not a proving command
findings.finding: AC2 partly relies on 'code review' as a proving method ('verified by code review + the behavior tests'), which is a non-automated, non-deterministic hedge. The structural claim — that vendor link-type names are NEVER interpreted by core (only carried as opaque passengers) — has no standalone automated assertion; it is only implicitly covered if the behavior tests happen to catch every such interpretation path.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'Link diffing compares canonical relations only; vendor link-type names appear in core solely as opaque payload passengers — verified by code review + the behavior tests'
scenarios: A future contributor adds a conditional on opaque_vendor_type inside outbound_links.py; the behavior tests still pass because the payloads are identical but the structural invariant is violated — the only gate would have been the code review that never runs in CI.
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC2 text, drop 'code review' and state: 'verified by the behavior tests in Testing (ADD/REMOVE payloads identical to today) and by the AST gate confirming no adapters.jira import (preventing any vendor-shape branching at the import level).' This makes all verification automated and CI-enforced.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit proving command for the capability-threading claim in Remediation AC5 — e.g., a unit test that calls `compute_outbound_mutations` with a mock backend and asserts that `map_remote_links` / `link_payload_for_relation` on that mock are invoked, or note that the behavior tests in Testing section serve this role and cross-reference them explicitly.
evidence: 'verified by … the AST neutrality gate still passes for outbound_differ.py (the capability is the injected backend, not an adapters.jira import)'
ABSENCE: no stated test or command confirms that compute_outbound_mutations actually threads the capability as the 4th argument to _diff_links
findings.finding: Remediation AC5 states two claims — (a) `outbound_differ.py` passes the `SupportsLinks` capability into `_diff_links`, and (b) the AST neutrality gate still passes — but the only stated proving command is the AST gate, which verifies (b) only. Claim (a) (the capability is actually threaded through) is not directly proven; it is at best implied by the behavior tests passing, but that link is not made explicit.
findings.location: Plan-review remediation — Acceptance Criteria: 'outbound_differ.py passes the SupportsLinks capability into _diff_links; the AST neutrality gate still passes for outbound_differ.py'
scenarios: An implementer adds the import removal but forgets to thread the capability argument; the AST gate passes, linting passes, but _diff_links falls back to a default or raises at runtime. No AC catches this until end-to-end integration.
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC5 to read: '…; verified by the behavior tests (which call through compute_outbound_mutations with a stubbed backend — if the capability were not threaded, map_remote_links would never be called and the ADD/REMOVE payloads would be wrong) AND the AST neutrality gate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the Approach section's 'compare … in relation vocabulary' language with the remediation's explicit `(opaque_vendor_type, remote_key)` ADD-dedup key — update the Approach text to state that ADD matching uses `(vendor_type, remote_key)` while REMOVE matching uses the canonical `(relation, remote_key)` pair.
evidence: Approach: 'Core _diff_links/_diff_link_removals … then compare local ticket["deps"] … against the canonical set in relation vocabulary'

Remediation: 'the core ADD dedup matches a local dep against a canonical entry on (opaque_vendor_type, remote_key) — the direction-agnostic pair equal to today's (type_name, other_key) — NOT on (relation, remote_key)'
findings.finding: The Approach section says core compares local deps against the canonical set 'in relation vocabulary', implying ADD dedup on (relation, remote_key), but the Plan-review remediation section explicitly specifies the ADD dedup key as (opaque_vendor_type, remote_key) — two contradictory matching keys for the same ADD dedup step that would send the implementer in two directions.
findings.location: Approach section ('compare local ticket["deps"] against the canonical set in relation vocabulary') vs Plan-review remediation section ('the core ADD dedup matches a local dep against a canonical entry on (opaque_vendor_type, remote_key) — NOT on (relation, remote_key)')
scenarios: Implementer reads Approach and writes ADD dedup on (relation, remote_key); fails direction-agnostic test added by remediation AC1.
Implementer reads remediation and writes ADD dedup on (opaque_vendor_type, remote_key); conflicts with Approach text 'in relation vocabulary'.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, replace 'compare local ticket["deps"] against the canonical set in relation vocabulary' with: 'For ADD dedup, core calls `link_payload_for_relation(relation)` to obtain `(vendor_type, swap)` and compares `(vendor_type, target_key)` against `(opaque_vendor_type, remote_key)` entries in the canonical set (direction-agnostic). For REMOVE matching, the canonical entry's `relation` field is used with the managed-ref gate (direction-specific, mirroring today's resolve_inbound_link semantics).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update AC2 to remove 'solely' and clarify the two roles of vendor type names in core: (1) as the ADD dedup match key via `(opaque_vendor_type, remote_key)`, and (2) as a REMOVE payload passenger — both consistent with the remediation's direction-agnostic requirement.
evidence: AC2: 'vendor link-type names appear in core solely as opaque payload passengers — verified by code review + the behavior tests'
Remediation: 'the core ADD dedup matches a local dep against a canonical entry on (opaque_vendor_type, remote_key) — NOT on (relation, remote_key)'

Remediation: 'link_payload_for_relation(relation) yields the local dep's (vendor_type, swap), and the dedup compares that vendor_type + target key against the canonical entries' (opaque_vendor_type, remote_key)'
findings.finding: Original AC2 states 'vendor link-type names appear in core solely as opaque payload passengers' (i.e., only in the REMOVE payload), but the remediation AC1 and its explanation require core to actively use `opaque_vendor_type` as a matching key in the ADD dedup — making vendor type names materially present in core diffing logic, not 'solely' as passengers. These two acceptance criteria directly contradict each other.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section, AC2 ('vendor link-type names appear in core solely as opaque payload passengers') vs Plan-review remediation AC1 ('The ADD dedup is direction-agnostic … dedups against a remote link of the mapped vendor type')
scenarios: A code reviewer uses AC2 to reject an implementation where core reads opaque_vendor_type for dedup, even though that is exactly what the remediation requires.
A code reviewer uses AC2's 'solely as opaque payload passengers' to pass an implementation that does relation-based ADD dedup, which fails the remediation's direction-agnostic test.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'vendor link-type names appear in core solely as opaque payload passengers' with 'vendor link-type names are never directly imported in core; they appear only as (a) the opaque matching key in the direction-agnostic ADD dedup (`(opaque_vendor_type, remote_key)`) and (b) the REMOVE payload type passenger — in both cases carried opaquely through the canonical entry, never via an adapters.jira import.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit statement enumerating all current `SupportsLinks` implementors and either (a) confirm Jira is the only one (making the addition safe) or (b) list the expand-contract steps for updating other adapters; and add a back-out note (e.g., the two new members can be removed and the vendor import restored to outbound_links.py as a single revert).
evidence: Approach: 'Move both link translations onto the SupportsLinks capability (adapter-side, translation only — neither member is parameterized on a shape a non-Jira backend lacks)'
Scope lists only 'adapters/jira/backend.py (implement the two members)' — no other backend is mentioned
No rollback path or expand-contract sequence is described for the protocol change
findings.finding: Adding `map_remote_links` and `link_payload_for_relation` as new required members to the `SupportsLinks` protocol/capability is a breaking interface change for any existing non-Jira adapter that implements `SupportsLinks`. The plan acknowledges non-Jira backends exist ('neither member is parameterized on a shape a non-Jira backend lacks') but provides no expand-contract sequence, no rollback path, and no explicit list of other implementors that must be updated.
findings.location: Approach section ('Move both link translations onto the SupportsLinks capability'); Scope section (only jira/backend.py listed as implementor)
scenarios: A non-Jira adapter implementing SupportsLinks fails at runtime (Protocol) or class-definition time (ABC) after this change lands, with no migration path specified.
A rollback of this change requires simultaneously un-implementing the two members from the Jira adapter and restoring the vendor import in outbound_links.py — no back-out sequence is documented.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an update to ADR 0035 (or a companion design note) documenting the two new `SupportsLinks` members — their return-tuple shapes, the `relation=None` semantics for unmapped remote link types, and the direction-agnostic dedup invariant — and list that doc update in Scope.
evidence: Plan references 'ADR 0035 §(d)' as authority for the boundary pattern but lists no update to that ADR in Scope

New protocol members `map_remote_links` → `(relation, remote_key, opaque_vendor_type)` and `link_payload_for_relation` → `(vendor_type, swap)` with `relation=None` semantics are specified only in plan prose
Direction-agnostic dedup invariant (match on `(opaque_vendor_type, remote_key)`) is a new adapter contract specified only in the remediation section
ABSENCE: no ADR update, AGENTS.md edit, or design-doc file appears in Scope
findings.finding: The plan adds two new members (`map_remote_links`, `link_payload_for_relation`) to the `SupportsLinks` capability protocol and codifies a direction-agnostic dedup contract (keyed on `(opaque_vendor_type, remote_key)`), but does not schedule an update to ADR 0035 or any AGENTS.md/design doc to capture these new adapter-facing contracts. Any future adapter author must know what these methods must return (tuple shape, `relation=None` semantics, direction-agnostic dedup invariant), yet that contract lives only in the plan prose after merge.
scenarios: A developer implementing a second backend (e.g., GitHub Issues) consults `SupportsLinks` and finds no documented contract for what `map_remote_links` must return, leading to incorrect `relation=None` handling or wrong tuple arity
ADR 0035 describes the canonical-shape boundary principle but omits the specific capability members; a future reviewer sees a drift between the ADR and the code
findings.suggested_fix: In Scope, add an entry such as `docs/adr/0035-canonical-link-shape.md` (update §capability-members). In the ADR, add a table: `map_remote_links(remote_fields) → list[tuple[relation|None, remote_key, opaque_vendor_type]]` and `link_payload_for_relation(relation) → tuple[vendor_type, swap]`, with the `relation=None` drop rule and the direction-agnostic dedup key `(opaque_vendor_type, remote_key)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a specified test (and a documented return-value contract) for `link_payload_for_relation(relation)` when `relation` is not in the adapter's map — confirm core skips the ADD without crashing, and that the behavior is pinned by a unit test.
evidence: Plan specifies: '`link_payload_for_relation(relation)` → `(vendor_type, swap)` — the emission translation, replacing the inline `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK.get(relation)` at `outbound_links.py:105`'
Testing section covers unknown *remote* link types via `map_remote_links` (relation=None), but has no coverage for `link_payload_for_relation` receiving an unknown local relation
ABSENCE: no test scenario or AC specifies the return value / core behavior when `link_payload_for_relation` receives a relation not in the adapter map
findings.finding: `link_payload_for_relation(relation)` has no specified test for the unknown-relation case (relation not in the adapter's map). Today `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK.get(relation)` returns `None` at line 105; if the new method also returns `None`, the core ADD path must handle it without crashing — but neither the contract (what the method returns for an unmapped relation) nor a test covering that path is specified anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Testing section; `link_payload_for_relation` contract in Approach; `outbound_links.py:105` context
scenarios: A local dep carries a relation string not present in `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` (e.g. from a future relation type or a data-migration artifact). `link_payload_for_relation` returns None/raises; core either crashes or silently drops the dep with no coverage confirming which.

If the contract is 'return None and core skips the ADD', that skip-on-unknown behavior is a behavioral guarantee that should be pinned by a test — otherwise a future refactor can change it undetected.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Testing section, add: 'Edge: `link_payload_for_relation` called with an unknown relation returns `None`; core emits no ADD for that dep and does not raise — verified by a unit test asserting an empty mutation list for a local dep whose relation has no Jira mapping.'

findings.finding: Plan-review remediation #1 asserts the call site is 3-argument — 'compute_outbound_mutations ... calls _diff_links(ticket, jira_fields, binding_store)' and 'must additionally pass the SupportsLinks capability as the new 4th argument' — but outbound_differ.py already calls the 4-argument form with the links capability already threaded and injected.

The Context/Problem's load-bearing premises about the CURRENT codebase are refuted by the files as they exist: outbound_links.py has NO adapters.jira import (line 30 is a comment header, not an import); there is no _existing_jira_links function in outbound_links.py (it already lives in JiraBackend.map_remote_links at backend.py:217-255); and _diff_links already translates via links.link_payload_for_relation (outbound_links.py:83), not inline at line 105 (which emits the ADD payload). The described vendor-shaped 'today' does not match the code.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/architecture.md` to Scope and update line 328 to replace `_existing_jira_links` with a reference to `JiraBackend.map_remote_links` (the absorbing implementation) so the architecture doc matches the post-change module structure.
evidence: docs/architecture.md:328: '(`_existing_jira_links`/`_diff_links`/`_diff_link_removals` + the relation maps) to `outbound_links.py`'
`_existing_jira_links` does not appear anywhere in outbound_links.py after the change — it was absorbed into JiraBackend.map_remote_links
docs/architecture.md is absent from the plan's Scope list
findings.finding: `docs/architecture.md` line 328 names `_existing_jira_links` as part of the `outbound_links.py` link-diff cluster, but this function is eliminated by the plan (absorbed into `JiraBackend.map_remote_links`). The file is not listed in Scope, leaving a stale reference that contradicts the post-change module structure.
scenarios: A developer reading docs/architecture.md after the change looks for `_existing_jira_links` in outbound_links.py and finds it does not exist, causing confusion about the module's structure.
findings.suggested_fix: In docs/architecture.md line 328, replace `_existing_jira_links`/`_diff_links`/`_diff_link_removals` + the relation maps` with `_diff_links`/`_diff_link_removals` (relation translations moved to `JiraBackend.map_remote_links`/`link_payload_for_relation`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `adapters/jira/acli_graph.py` to Scope and update its `get_issuelinks_map` docstring (line 392) to reference `JiraBackend.map_remote_links` instead of the now-removed `outbound_differ._existing_jira_links`.
evidence: acli_graph.py:392: 'differ's dedup (``outbound_differ._existing_jira_links``) read'
`_existing_jira_links` is eliminated by this plan and does not appear in outbound_links.py or outbound_differ.py after the change
acli_graph.py is not listed in the plan's Scope
findings.finding: `adapters/jira/acli_graph.py` line 392 references `outbound_differ._existing_jira_links` in a docstring comment explaining the Bug 3f04 fix. After this plan eliminates `_existing_jira_links`, the comment names a non-existent symbol. This file is not in Scope.
scenarios: A developer tracing the Bug 3f04 fix reads the `get_issuelinks_map` docstring, follows the `outbound_differ._existing_jira_links` reference, and finds neither the attribute nor the module export exists.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
